body_txt: You gotta face it: no matter how happy you are to be on that awards stand, first place is still better than second. But sometimes a person takes this a bit too far. When somebody starts acting like second place is for losers, it doesn't matter if you were in a race against everyone on earth and beat 7,999,999,998 people. All that matters is you didn't beat the one. Essentially it's being a Sore Loser , just, y'know, for those who only "lost" very slightly. Subtrope of The Perfectionist . Some real-life athletes feel this way quite deeply, coming so close and yet missing the mark. For example, one scientific study analyzed photographs of the facial expressions of many Olympians as they learned how they had placed. Gold of course was almost always ecstatic, and Bronze medalists were happy to have placed at all, but a Silver winner usually showed disappointment or dejection, unless it was their first time competing. This is especially true in tournament-style sports like basketball and hockey, where the bronze team just won their final game, but the silver medalists lost the game for gold. And the medal ceremony takes place immediately afterwards, giving the runners-up no time to compose themselves and put on a sporting face. This trope is also well-known in the entertainment industry and its top awards, where it's a running joke that anyone who says "It's an honor just to be nominated" is lying their asses off. This is despite that fact that even getting nominated for an Oscar, Grammy, and so on is a career honor most performers never see, but one can forgive a little frustration when a performer is nominated year after year for decades without ever winning . In a narrative sense, this can be used to give An Aesop about sportsmanship, or can be used to squeeze some extra angst from a character, especially if the second-placing character was hoping to use the competition to impress a "Well Done, Son" Guy parent. This trope is the prime reason Underdogs Never Lose . Note this trope is only about characters getting second place, or at least placing highly enough to get a medal/trophy/etc., and then focusing on the fact that they still didn't win first. It's NOT about: Compare Unsportsmanlike Gloating , The B Grade , Losing Is Worse Than Death ; cases where there is intent to make a person feel bad about placing second by those in first place and or outside factions involved. If it happens all the time, it can become a case of Always Second Best . See also Rejecting the Consolation Prize , which could be the end result. Contrast The Runner-Up Takes It All , Sportsmanship Aesop , Second Place Is for Winners . Compare and contrast Disqualification-Induced Victory . See also Medal of Dishonor and Damned by Faint Praise . Sub-Trope of Second Prize , when a character was expecting a different outcome in a competition. Not to be confused with Tough Act to Follow . Please note that many real-life leagues (especially youth leagues) consider this attitude very bad sportsmanship . No Real Life Examples, Please!
